growth_records.cite: Foster et al. Modeling Growth Kinetics, Interspecies Cell Fusion, and Metabolism of a Clostridium acetobutylicum/Clostridium ljungdahlii Syntrophic Coculture. mSystems (2021). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.01325-20. [PMC8573953]
growth_records.snippet: Clj nonhybrid (iJL680) 539 453 iJL680 0.22 ... maximum growth rate using 5 mmol gDW−1 h−1 fructose as the sole carbon source
growth_records.notes: Model (FBA) MAXIMUM growth rate, not experimental: iJL680 gave mu=0.22 h-1 with imposed fructose uptake 5 mmol/gDW/h (sole carbon). doubling_time = ln2/0.22 = 3.15 h (calculated). Hybrid variant gave 0.24 h-1. Note after 15 h in coculture the Cac growth rate was reported an order of magnitude lower than that of Clj. Low confidence: theoretical GEM output.

growth_records.cite: Zhou, Guangye et al. Bioadaptive Ni single atoms unlock high rate microbial electrosynthesis of isopropanol from CO2. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-68358-8. [PMC12905360]
growth_records.snippet: CO-supplemented fermentation produced ~11.4 mM IPA (681.3 mg/L), representing a 142-times enhancement, which was also accompanied by a 1.7 times (73.9 mM, 4359.3 mg/L) increase in acetate and a 3.3 times boost (29.3 mM, 1347.8 mg/L) in etha
growth_records.notes: Batch gas fermentation, CO/H2/CO2 (50/20/30) feeding, 37 C, YT medium. Values are FINAL titers (not gDW-normalized specific rates). Titers reported both in mM and mg/L in text. No growth rate given: authors state OD600 became unreliable because dead cells remained in suspension after day 3-5; only CFU/mL monitored, no numeric mu reported. Gas substrate (CO/H2/CO2) consumption not quantified, so uptake_rates left empty. medium composition from Methods: 10 g/L Bacto yeast extract, 16 g/L Bacto tryptone, 4 g/L NaCl, 0.5 g/L cysteine-HCl; 5 g/L fructose was added ONLY when reviving frozen stocks (not in fermentation medium). pH from Methods/text: bioelectrolyte pH approx 6.

growth_records.strain: Engineered IPA-producing strain (overexpressing AACT and AADC; from Lo et al., ref 17)
growth_records.medium: YT bioelectrolyte (integrated microbial electrochemical system, iMES)

sec.method: GC/HPLC of bioelectrolyte, daily sampling over 4-day run

growth_records.cite: Zhou, Guangye et al. Bioadaptive Ni single atoms unlock high rate microbial electrosynthesis of isopropanol from CO2. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-68358-8. [PMC12905360]
growth_records.snippet: The hybrid iMES system achieved an IPA production rate of 161.3 mg/L/day (10.8 A/m2) under ambient conditions ... we achieved the CO-mediated CO2-to-IPA microbial electrochemical synthesis with an average rate of 161.3 mg/L/day
growth_records.notes: Distinct condition: CO2 microbial electrosynthesis (iMES biohybrid) rather than bottled syngas fermentation; CO donor generated electrochemically in situ. IPA volumetric productivity 161.3 mg/L/day at 10.8 A/m2, -0.75 V vs RHE, 37 C, 4-day operation, initial OD600 ~0.5. Volumetric (per-litre) rate, not gDW-normalized; no biomass-specific rate or growth rate reported. Bioelectrolyte = YT medium; ion chromatography gave 93.3 mM Na+ and 13.3 mM K+ (Supplementary Table S1). Note a cited comparison (ref 64) reports ~116.7 mg/L/day PHB with ~100 mg/L/day IPA byproduct using a different strain/pathway (NOT this organism).

growth_records.cite: Lo, Jonathan et al. Thermodynamic and Kinetic Modeling Directs Pathway Optimization for Isopropanol Production in a Gas-Fermenting Bacterium. mSystems (2023). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01274-22. [PMC10134883]
growth_records.method: reported OD600 only (peak OD600 = 3.48 at day 3); no specific growth rate reported and none derivable without OD-to-gDW conversion
growth_records.snippet: mixotrophic growth ... highest optical density of 3.48 achieved at day 3 ... final IPA titers ... 74 ± 1.3 mM (4.44 ± 0.078 g/L). Acetate was the major product, which reached 117 mM, after 7 days. Both ethanol and 3-hydroxybutyrate (3HB) ..
growth_records.notes: Engineered gas-fermenting acetogen. Products are HPLC-measured end-point TITERS (mM / g/L), not specific rates in mmol/gDW/h; biomass reported only as OD600 (peak 3.48 at day 3) with no OD-to-gDW factor, so per-biomass rates are not derivable. All secreted products (IPA, acetate, ethanol, 3HB) are experimental. Fructose (20 g/L) and syngas are supplied substrates but consumption rates not quantified (is_experimental=false as rates). Earlier construct titers reported in same paper (context, not separate records): IPA v1 0.78 g/L IPA + 0.05 g/L acetone; IPA v2 0.97 g/L; ctfAB+aadc 2.7 g/L; atoAD+aadc 1.7 g/L in YTF.

growth_records.snippet: grew exponentially, reaching the highest optical density (OD) of around 4 to 5 at day 13 ... IPA production reached the maximum at day 12 ... peak titer (Titer_max) of 39.7 mM (2.39 g/L) ... acetate ... reached its highest level (80.1 mM; C
growth_records.notes: Autotrophic (gas-only) 2-L bioreactor run of same engineered strain. HPLC end-point titers (mM / g/L), not per-biomass rates; biomass only as OD600 (~4-5 at day 13), no gDW conversion so rates not derivable. All secreted products experimental. Syngas uptake not quantified. pH controlled at 5.2, 37°C, 300 sccm gas sparge. Carbon partitioning: IPA 29%, acetate 34% of product carbon.

growth_records.cite: Florian Oswald et al. Formic Acid Formation by Clostridium ljungdahlii at Elevated Pressures of Carbon Dioxide and Hydrogen. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2018.00006. [PMC5816570]
growth_records.snippet: Set-up 1.5 L: formic acid 0.03 +/- 0.00, acetic acid 9.30 +/- 2.30, EtOH 2.81 +/- 0.13 g L-1; cH2,R 1.00 mol L-1, cCO2,R 0.40 mol L-1; EH2 25.63%, ECO2 21.52%; qH2,max 2.40, qCO2,max 1.00 mmol min-1 g-1 (Table 1, after 90 h).
growth_records.notes: Baseline 1.5 L STR, 1 bar absolute, 37C, pH 5.9, anaerobic batch, gas-sparged (H2/CO2/N2). Prokaryote acetogen. Growth rate NOT reported (OD not quantified; 'no significant increase in OD at elevated pressures'), so mu/td left null. Uptake unit conversion: mmol/min/g x 60 = mmol/gDW/h. qH2,max=2.40 -> 144; qCO2,max=1.00 -> 60 mmol/gDW/h. cH2,R/cCO2,R are cumulative consumed per L reactor (not rates). Yps 0.67 g/g (per consumed), 0.15 g/g (per fed) reported in Results/Discussion. Products are endpoint titers at 90 h; volumetric productivities computed as titer/90 h shown in units field. Medium base = Tanner 2007; full composition by reference (Oswald et al. 2016); components explicitly given HERE: 0.33 g/L NH4Cl, 1 g/L Cys-HCl.H2O, 5 g/L fructose, pH 5.9 (KOH).

growth_records.cite: Florian Oswald et al. Formic Acid Formation by Clostridium ljungdahlii at Elevated Pressures of Carbon Dioxide and Hydrogen. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2018.00006. [PMC5816570]
growth_records.notes: 2.5 L high-pressure STR at 1 bar (HPF-1), 37C, pH 5.9, anaerobic gas-fed batch. Uptake conversion mmol/min/g x60: qH2,max 4.56->273.6, qCO2,max 1.05->63 mmol/gDW/h (H2 SD very large, replicates diverge). Consumed amounts (cH2,R/cCO2,R) ~1/3 of 1.5 L; consumption ratios EH2/ECO2 ~half. Growth rate NOT reported (mu/td null). Yps per consumed 0.64 g/g, per fed 0.05 g/g. B. cereus contamination detected in all HPF cultivations but only on carried-over fructose; blank (no C. ljungdahlii) gave no growth/products, so acetogenic products attributed to C. ljungdahlii. Volumetric productivities = titer/90 h.

growth_records.cite: Florian Oswald et al. Formic Acid Formation by Clostridium ljungdahlii at Elevated Pressures of Carbon Dioxide and Hydrogen. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2018.00006. [PMC5816570]
growth_records.snippet: HPF-4: formic 1.34 +/- 0.28, acetic 1.90 +/- 0.36, EtOH 0.20 +/- 0.03 g L-1; consumption N/A. Overall Y_P/S 0.04 g g-1. Product spectrum shifts toward formic acid with increasing pressure (Table 1, 90 h).
growth_records.notes: 2.5 L high-pressure STR at 4 bar absolute (HPF-4), 37C, pH 5.9, anaerobic gas-fed batch, 90 h, n=2 reactors. Consumed-substrate and specific uptake rates N/A (off-gas resembled saturation curves at elevated pressure, no reliable mass balance). Growth rate NOT reported (no OD increase at elevated pressure). Product spectrum shifts to formic acid vs 1 bar. Overall Yps(fed) = 0.04 g/g. Volumetric productivities = titer/90 h. Same B. cereus contamination caveat as other HPF runs (blank yielded no products).

growth_records.cite: Caivano et al. Enzyme-constrained metabolic model and in silico metabolic engineering of Clostridium ljungdahlii for the development of sustainable production processes. Computational and Structural Biotechnology Journal (2023). DOI:10.1016/j.csbj.2023.09.015. [PMC10543971]
growth_records.snippet: Fructose, CO2 -0.672, -15.861 ... 0.110 [This study] ... acetate was nearly the sole secreted product ... compared to an experimental value of 6.79 mmol/gCDW/h ... largely deviating from the experimental value of 0.0035 mmol/gCDW/h
growth_records.notes: THIS-STUDY experimental mixotrophic batch culture #1. Uptake rates from Table 2 row 'Fructose, CO2 -0.672, -15.861 ... 0.110 [This study]' (fructose 0.672, CO2 15.861 mmol/gCDW/h consumed; growth 0.110 h-1). Doubling time computed td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.110=6.30 h. Secretion acetate 6.79 and ethanol 0.0035 mmol/gCDW/h are the experimental values the paper compares model predictions against for this mixotrophic condition. H2 uptake was NOT measured (paper states experimental H2 uptake unknown); do not treat as experimental. Two fructose concentrations (5 and 10 g/L) tested; specific concentration per culture not stated.

growth_records.snippet: the biokinetic parameters were obtained as μmax = 0.195 h-1 ... The maximum specific CO uptake rate (qmax = 34.364 mmol/gcell/h), CO inhibition constant (KI = 0.601 atm)
growth_records.notes: Batch serum-bottle condition at 0.2 atm initial synthesis-gas pressure. growth_rate_per_h=0.153 h-1 is Volterra-model mu_m fitted to experimental cell-density (OD580->CDW) data (Table 1, R2=0.972); doubling_time_h=ln2/mu=0.693/0.153=4.53 h. Ethanol & acetate Rmax volumetric rates and Pmax from modified Gompertz fit to measured concentrations (Table 4). CO uptake qmax=34.364 mmol/gcell/h is a single global Andrews-model parameter (not per-pressure). Note: cited H2 uptake q=0.079 mol/gcell/h and mu=0.06 h-1 are from Phillips et al. (ref 5, another paper), NOT measured here; P.productus qmax=144 mmol/gcell/h is from Vega et al. (ref 9). Substrate conversion (this study) peaked at 1.0 atm: 76% H2, 67% CO.

growth_records.cite: Mohammadi, Maedeh et al. Kinetic Studies on Fermentative Production of Biofuel from Synthesis Gas Using Clostridium ljungdahlii. The Scientific World Journal (2014). DOI:10.1155/2014/910590. [PMC3925604]
growth_records.snippet: The highest substrate conversion efficiency of 76% for H2 and 67% for CO was achieved at the initial synthesis gas pressure of 1.0 atm ... mu_m=0.196 h-1 (R2=0.987); ethanol Rmax=0.1722 mmol/L/h
growth_records.notes: 1.0 atm initial gas pressure = optimum. mu=0.196 h-1 (peak, Table 1); td=0.693/0.196=3.54 h. Highest ethanol Rmax (0.1722 mmol/L/h) and highest ethanol+acetate Pmax. Measured conversion 76% H2, 67% CO. H2 consumed preferentially over CO (homoacetic pathway).

growth_records.cite: Mohammadi, Maedeh et al. Kinetic Studies on Fermentative Production of Biofuel from Synthesis Gas Using Clostridium ljungdahlii. The Scientific World Journal (2014). DOI:10.1155/2014/910590. [PMC3925604]
growth_records.snippet: Table 1 (1.2 atm): mu_m=0.145 h-1, R2=0.991; Table 4 ethanol Rmax=0.0672, acetate Rmax=0.0618 mmol/L/h
growth_records.notes: 1.2 atm initial gas pressure. mu=0.145 h-1 Volterra fit; td=0.693/0.145=4.78 h. Growth declines above 1.0 atm due to CO inhibition (Sm,CO=0.743 atm dissolved-CO no-growth threshold). Rmax volumetric from Gompertz fit.

growth_records.snippet: C. ljungdahlii ERI-2 (ATCC 55380) | Two-stage cascade | CSTR pH 5.5-5.7 growth | Bubble column pH 4.4-4.8 cell+gas recycle | Syngas 60%CO/35%H2/5%CO2 | Ethanol 450 mM, 0.37 g/L/h
growth_records.notes: SECONDARY DATA compiled in review Table; original citation: Richter H, Martin M, Angenent L. A two-stage continuous fermentation system for conversion of syngas into ethanol. Energies. 2013;6:3987–4000. doi: 10.3390/en60. Solventogenic/acetogenic Clostridium, anaerobic. Dilution rate D reported; growth rate NOT equal to D (multi-stage / cell-retention / immobilized -> growth uncoupled from D). Volumetric productivity (g/L/h) and yield (g/g) are the reported measured rates.

Volumetric productivity (g/L/h) and yield (g/g) are the reported measured rates. Cell-retention continuous reactor: dilution rate D uncoupled from specific growth rate mu. Biomass (CDW/OD) 10-18 g/L; bleed rate bleed for constant CDW.

growth_records.cite: Richter, Hanno et al. A Narrow pH Range Supports Butanol, Hexanol, and Octanol Production from Syngas in a Continuous Co-culture of Clostridium ljungdahlii and Clostridium kluyveri with In-Line Product Extraction. Frontiers in Microbiology (2016). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2016.01773. [PMC5099930]
growth_records.snippet: During Phase 1 and 2, the pH in the reactor was 6.0 with either a high dilution rate (80 mL/h) or a low dilution rate (40 mL/h)... Table 1 Balances for substrates and products during phases of steady-state.
growth_records.notes: Continuous co-culture syngas fermentation (Frontiers Microbiol 2016). Phase 1: pH 6.0, media feed 80 mL/h into 1 L working volume => dilution rate D=0.08/h. Growth rate NOT measured directly; paper states steady-state mu = cell bleed rate = 1/4 x D = 0.020/h; doubling time td=ln2/mu=34.7 h. Substrate CONSUMPTION rates (CO, H2) from Table 1 'Consumption (mmol C/L/d)'; note H2 carries no carbon so its value is effectively mmol H2/L/d despite the shared column header. Secretion rates are the net COMBINED (reactor effluent + stripping condensate) volumetric production rates from Table 1 'Prod. rate comb', unit mmol carbon/L/d (authors use mmol C to fairly weight longer chains). Rates are VOLUMETRIC not biomass-specific: only OD600 (5-10) was measured, no gDW biomass, so Yxs and qS in mmol/gDW/h are NOT derivable. Standard deviations (Table 1, N per phase): CO=2594+-500, H2=1106+-243; acetate=678+-93; ethanol=116+-48; n-butyrate=58.5+-30.2; n-caproate=22.3+-17.4; n-butanol=15.2+-8.0; n-hexanol=7.29+-4.0; n-octanol=0.0035+-0.0012; 2,3-butanediol=32.0+-12.3; CO2=710+-286 mmol C/L/d. product_yields are the paper's % carbon-recovery selectivity (mol C in product / mol C in CO consumed). Medium = 2x the mineral medium of Mock et al.; stock g/L values are for the mineral-salt stock combined at 60 mL + 20 mL vitamins + 20 mL trace elements per L final; full vitamin stock (per L): 10 mg pyridoxine-HCl, 50 mg each thiamine/riboflavin/Ca-pantothenate/thioctic acid/pABA/nicotinic acid/B12, 20 mg biotin, 20 mg folic acid, 10 mg Mesna; trace-element stock (per L): 2 g NTA, 25.2 mg MgCl2, 2.84 g (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2, 47.6 mg CoCl2, 200 mg ZnSO4.7H2O, 119 mg NiCl2.6H2O, 48.4 mg Na2MoO4, 34.6 mg Na2SeO3, 66 mg Na2WO4.2H2O; plus 1 mM cysteine and continuous Na2S feed giving ~1 mM (feed 80 mL/h) or ~2 mM (feed 40 mL/h) H2S. N=1 sample per day; averages over N=5 days of steady state.

Secretion rates are the net COMBINED (reactor effluent + stripping condensate) volumetric production rates from Table 1 'Prod. rate comb', unit mmol carbon/L/d (authors use mmol C to fairly weight longer chains). Rates are VOLUMETRIC not biomass-specific: only OD600 (5-10) was measured, no gDW biomass, so Yxs and qS in mmol/gDW/h are NOT derivable. Standard deviations (Table 1, N per phase): CO=1893+-78, H2=881+-122; acetate=548+-143; ethanol=144.7+-61.4; n-butyrate=58.2+-11.6; n-caproate=22.1+-6.8; n-butanol=23.6+-4.8; n-hexanol=5.04+-1.82; n-octanol=0.0557+-0.027; 2,3-butanediol=3.1+-0.3; CO2=623+-139 mmol C/L/d. product_yields are the paper's % carbon-recovery selectivity (mol C in product / mol C in CO consumed). Medium = 2x the mineral medium of Mock et al.; stock g/L values are for the mineral-salt stock combined at 60 mL + 20 mL vitamins + 20 mL trace elements per L final; full vitamin stock (per L): 10 mg pyridoxine-HCl, 50 mg each thiamine/riboflavin/Ca-pantothenate/thioctic acid/pABA/nicotinic acid/B12, 20 mg biotin, 20 mg folic acid, 10 mg Mesna; trace-element stock (per L): 2 g NTA, 25.2 mg MgCl2, 2.84 g (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2, 47.6 mg CoCl2, 200 mg ZnSO4.7H2O, 119 mg NiCl2.6H2O, 48.4 mg Na2MoO4, 34.6 mg Na2SeO3, 66 mg Na2WO4.2H2O; plus 1 mM cysteine and continuous Na2S feed giving ~1 mM (feed 80 mL/h) or ~2 mM (feed 40 mL/h) H2S. N=1 sample per day; averages over N=5 days of steady state.

growth_records.medium: 2x modified Mock et al. mineral medium (syngas)

up.method: syngas (60% CO/35% H2/5% CO2) consumed; per-phase startup value not tabulated separately
syngas H2 consumed; startup value not separately tabulated

sec.method: HPLC; startup pure-culture main product
HPLC; up-to value during startup
HPLC/GC; startup

growth_records.cite: Richter, Hanno et al. A Narrow pH Range Supports Butanol, Hexanol, and Octanol Production from Syngas in a Continuous Co-culture of Clostridium ljungdahlii and Clostridium kluyveri with In-Line Product Extraction. Frontiers in Microbiology (2016). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2016.01773. [PMC5099930]
growth_records.snippet: During the startup phase with only C. ljungdahlii, we observed ethanol production rates of up to 170 mmol C/L/d, but with acetate as the main product (721 mmol C/L/d) and some 2,3-butanediol (52 mmol C/L/d).
growth_records.notes: Pure-culture C. ljungdahlii PETC syngas fermentation during reactor startup at pH 6.0, continuous feed 80 mL/h in 1 L (D=0.08/h); mu estimated = 1/4 x D = 0.02/h (cell bleed = 1/4 dilution), td=ln2/0.02=34.7 h. Values are in-text approximate ('up to') volumetric production rates in mmol carbon/L/d, not from a table, hence medium confidence; no SDs given. Volumetric not biomass-specific (OD only). CO/H2 uptake for this startup subphase not separately reported. Syngas feed 60% CO/35% H2/5% CO2.

growth_records.medium: not specified in review

up.method: maximum biomass-specific CO uptake rate (kinetic parameter), cited from Mohammadi et al. (2014)

growth_records.cite: Kerkhof et al. Understanding microbial syngas fermentation rates. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol (2024). DOI:10.1007/s00253-024-13364-3. [PMC11662053]
growth_records.snippet: Table 3: Clostridium ljungdahlii qCO,max=-34.4 mmol/(gx.h), Ks,CO=0.017 mmol/L, KI,CO=0.50 mmol/L; Source Mohammadi et al. (2014).
growth_records.notes: qCO,max (maximum specific CO uptake) = -34.4 mmol_CO/(gx.h); Ks,CO=0.017 mmol_CO/L; KI,CO=0.50 mmol_CO/L. Cited FROM Mohammadi et al. (2014) (review Table 3). C. aceticum value recalculated from qAcT,max via Eq.3 (Table 3 footnote a). Culture conditions (T, pH, medium, mode) not restated in this review.

growth_records.cite: Oliveira, Luis et al. Comparison of Syngas-Fermenting Clostridia in Stirred-Tank Bioreactors and the Effects of Varying Syngas Impurities. Microorganisms (2022). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms10040681. [PMC9032146]
growth_records.method: reported (max specific growth rate); doubling time calculated
growth_records.snippet: C. ljungdahlii (0.23 g L-1)... CO uptake rates... 6 mmol L-1 h-1 (C. ljungdahlii)... maximum CO conversions of ... 13.4% (C. ljungdahlii). The observed maximum specific growth rate (0.050 h-1) and maximum CO uptake rate (6 mmol L-1 h-1) of
growth_records.notes: Autotrophic reference batch. mu=0.050/h reported; doubling_time = ln2/0.050 = 13.86 h. CO uptake ~6 mmol/L/h VOLUMETRIC max (final CDW 0.23 g/L). Note: C. ljungdahlii run at reduced CO (200 mbar) with 400 mbar N2 balance, deliberately, as preliminary studies showed better growth/ethanol at low CO. Max CO conversion 13.4%. alcohol:acetate 0.86 g/g. Absolute acetate/ethanol titers not given in main text (in Tables S1-S3).

derived.qs_mu_note: pooled across studies/media — not a single chemostat series, so no maintenance model fitted
